mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Saturday, April 11th.

On the Breeze front, no new coverage has broken in the last 24 hours. Yesterday's briefing captured Lincoln Airport's enthusiastic inaugural cycle across Nebraska television stations and Las Vegas digital media, with that story having fully played out in those markets. Breeze remains in active expansion mode — the Raleigh-Durham crew base is operational, international service to Montego Bay and Punta Cana is live, new West Coast routes including Arcata, Eugene, Redmond, and Pasco launched in recent weeks, and there is no negative Breeze coverage in circulation this morning. With every major U.S. carrier now having completed a historic round of baggage fee increases this month, communications may want to evaluate whether this weekend creates an opening for proactive messaging before that angle loses news cycle momentum.

Now for industry and competitor news.

LoyaltyLobby reported today that Qatar Airways has confirmed 109 passenger departures from Doha on Saturday, the clearest operational signal yet that Middle East aviation is resuming after the Iran-U.S. ceasefire was announced April 8th, with Iraq, Syria, and Bahrain having reopened their airspace and Gulf carriers publicly targeting over 120 destinations by mid-June. For Breeze, the critical question is fuel pricing: jet fuel is running near $209 per barrel today — nearly double the pre-war level — and if Strait of Hormuz restrictions ease and prices begin declining, revenue management and finance should model the unit cost impact on Breeze's summer flying season.

TravelWise and Rolling Out reported today that American and Alaska Airlines are the final major carriers to raise checked baggage fees, with American's first bag now at $50 and Alaska's rising to $45 effective today, completing a month-long industry sweep that already included Delta, Southwest, United, and JetBlue, all citing Iran war fuel costs that have surged nearly 150 percent since late February. Breeze's communications and marketing team should evaluate whether today — the day Alaska completes the sweep — is the right moment for a proactive message about Breeze's pricing, since the earned media opportunity for this angle will fade quickly after this weekend.

W.T.O.C. in Savannah reported yesterday that Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and the F.A.A. unveiled a gaming-community-focused air traffic controller hiring campaign, with the hiring window opening April 17th and a goal of filling over 2,300 new trainee controller positions this fiscal year to address chronic staffing shortfalls across the National Airspace System. Breeze's point-to-point model depends on schedule integrity at secondary airports where controller coverage can be thin, and a sustained F.A.A. staffing buildout is a long-term structural positive for Breeze's operational reliability — operations should track which airports receive the biggest controller additions in this hiring class.

Hawaii media reported that Alaska Airlines and Hawaiian Airlines are completing their full passenger service system integration on April 22nd, just eleven days away, when travelers will be able to book, check in, and manage flights across both carriers on a single unified platform, marking the operational culmination of the 2024 acquisition. Network planning should brief leadership on whether the newly combined Alaska-Hawaiian footprint — covering Las Vegas, Orange County, and the Pacific — creates material new competitive pressure on Breeze's West Coast routes, and whether any gaps in their combined network represent expansion opportunities for Breeze.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
